content.problemStatement:
There are NNN light bulbs in a row, numbered 111 through NNN from left to right. Each bulb can be in one of the states "on" or "off". Initially, all the bulbs are "off". Each bulb is also connected to its (at most two) adjacent bulbs by wires.

You should reach a state described by a given a binary string SSS with length NNN ― for each valid iii, if the iii-th character of SSS is '1', the iii-th bulb should be "on", and if it is '0', the iii-th bulb should be "off". You may do that by performing the following steps in the given order:


Cut up to KKK of the N−1N-1N−1 wires between adjacent bulbs (possibly none of them).
Switch on some bulbs (possibly none). Here, whenever two bulbs are still connected by wires (directly or indirectly) and one of them gets switched on, then the other one also gets switched on.
Disconnect some bulbs from the electrical network. Here, disconnecting a bulb switches it off, but it does not affect any other bulbs ― if a bulb was switched on before this step and it was not disconnected in this step, it remains switched on afterwards.
Minimise the number of bulbs you need to disconnect in step 3 in order to reach the given final state of the bulbs.
constraints: the sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 5⋅1055 \cdot 10^55⋅105
content.inputFormat:
The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and KKK.
The second line contains a single string SSS.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing one integer ― the smallest number of bulbs you need to disconnect.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Example case 1: We can cut the wires between pairs of bulbs (2,3)(2, 3)(2,3) and (4,5)(4, 5)(4,5), switch on bulbs 111, 222 and 555, and then disconnect bulb 111.
